text: I rehearse important presentations multiple times before delivering them.
I prefer to speak off the cuff because rehearsed delivery feels stiff to me.
I map out dependencies between tasks so I can work on them in the most efficient order.
I pick up whichever task feels most natural in the moment rather than mapping dependencies.
I track recurring expenses and review them monthly to stay within my targets.
I rarely review past expenses because I trust my general sense of where I stand financially.
I archive completed work in labeled folders so I can find it years from now.
I rarely organize finished work because I'd rather move on to the next thing.
I draft and revise important emails before sending them.
I send emails as soon as I write them, trusting my first phrasing is good enough.
I build checklists for recurring processes so nothing gets skipped.
I handle recurring tasks differently each time based on what the situation calls for.
I keep a log of what I accomplish each day to maintain awareness of my productivity.
I gauge my productivity by how energized I feel, not by a tally of completed items.
I write down lessons from each project so I can apply them to the next one.
I absorb lessons from experience naturally without needing to write them down.
I schedule thinking time into my calendar rather than leaving reflection to chance.
My best thinking happens at unplanned moments, so I don't try to schedule it.
I establish clear criteria before evaluating options so my judgment stays consistent.
I evaluate options holistically in the moment rather than against predetermined criteria.
I update my to-do list throughout the day as new tasks surface.
I carry tasks in my head and address them as they come to mind.
I feel uneasy leaving the house without confirming I have everything I need.
I head out the door and figure out what I forgot along the way.
I pre-read materials before classes, workshops, or events so I can engage more deeply.
I show up to learning experiences fresh, without preparation, to keep my perspective unbiased.
I keep a master list of all ongoing projects and their current statuses.
I keep track of projects mentally and trust I'll remember what needs attention.
I set weekly review sessions to assess what went well and what I need to adjust.
I adjust course continuously rather than waiting for a formal review session.
I establish naming conventions for my files so I can search for them easily.
I name files whatever comes to mind and rely on search tools when I need them.
I think through the logical sequence of an argument before presenting it.
I develop my argument as I speak, discovering what I think in real time.
I proactively identify risks at the start of any new undertaking.
I address risks as they materialize rather than cataloging them in advance.
I confirm details and expectations before starting any collaborative task.
I dive into collaborative work and let expectations clarify themselves through action.
I protect focused work time by blocking it on my calendar so no one can interrupt it.
I stay available throughout the day because the best conversations happen unexpectedly.
I return borrowed items promptly because outstanding obligations weigh on me.
I return things when I remember, without feeling pressured by a specific timeline.
I rehearse difficult conversations in my mind before having them.
I enter difficult conversations open-ended, letting the dialogue shape what I say.
I maintain version histories of important documents so I can revert changes if needed.
I work on the current version and don't worry about preserving past iterations.
I track my goals with quantifiable metrics so I can see objective progress.
I assess progress by how things feel rather than by numerical metrics.
I prepare fallback options before negotiating or making requests.
I negotiate and make requests without scripted fallbacks, adapting to the flow of conversation.
I always carry items I might need — a charger, a pen, an umbrella — just in case.
I travel light and improvise with whatever is available if I need something.
I verify facts and proofread carefully before sharing information with others.
I share information as I learn it, refining my understanding in conversation.
I plan my route before leaving for an unfamiliar destination.
I set off toward an unfamiliar destination and navigate as I go.
I think about what tools or supplies I'll need before starting a project.
I start projects with what I have and acquire tools as the need becomes clear.
I keep my digital files organized in a clear folder hierarchy.
My digital files end up wherever is convenient; I search when I need something.
I take time to define the scope of a task before beginning work on it.
I let the scope of a task reveal itself as I work rather than defining it upfront.
I respect the boundaries of my own schedule and protect time for rest and recovery.
I keep working when I'm energized regardless of what my schedule says, and rest when the energy fades.
I value knowing exactly where I stand on a project at all times.
I'm comfortable with ambiguity about where a project stands as long as progress feels real.
I like to establish ground rules before starting a collaborative effort.
I prefer to let norms emerge organically as people work together.
I consider multiple possible outcomes before committing to a decision.
I commit to a decision and adjust quickly if the outcome isn't what I expected.
I front-load effort on projects so the final stages are smoother.
I ramp up effort as a project progresses, letting early stages stay loose and exploratory.
I maintain a reading list and work through it systematically.
I read whatever catches my interest without following a list.
I keep my passwords organized in a secure, systematic way.
I deal with passwords as they come up and reset them when I forget.
I believe that success comes from executing a clear vision with discipline.
I believe that success comes from staying open and responsive to emerging possibilities.
I avoid last-minute rushes by distributing work evenly across the available time.
I accept that last-minute rushes are part of my natural rhythm and often produce my best work.
I study the instructions for new software before using it.
I click around new software to figure it out through exploration.
I schedule appointments with buffer time between them to avoid feeling rushed.
I schedule things back-to-back and adjust on the fly if something runs over.
I periodically declutter my spaces to keep things functional and clear.
I declutter only when accumulation actually interferes with what I'm doing.
I note important dates and send reminders to myself well in advance.
I trust that important dates will come to mind when they need to.
I design my morning so the most important work gets done before distractions arise.
I let each morning develop its own rhythm instead of front-loading priority tasks.
I outline chapters or sections before I begin writing anything substantial.
I start writing and let the structure emerge from the material itself.
I keep spare supplies on hand so I never run out of essentials.
I buy supplies when I run out rather than stockpiling in advance.
I separate my work into distinct phases with clear transitions between them.
I blend research, creation, and revision together fluidly rather than working in distinct phases.
I identify the single most important task each day and make sure it gets done first.
I let the day's most important task reveal itself through my engagement rather than deciding it in advance.
I maintain a consistent filing system that anyone else could navigate.
My organizational system makes sense to me even if it would confuse someone else.
I follow up on requests I've made to ensure nothing falls through the cracks.
I trust that things will come back to me without chasing them down.
I devote a fixed portion of my income to savings regardless of circumstances.
I save when it feels comfortable and spend more freely when opportunities arise.
I hold the view that patience in execution is what turns a good idea into a finished product.
I hold the view that speed in execution is what keeps an idea alive long enough to matter.
I write detailed instructions when handing off work to others.
I give others a quick overview and trust them to figure out the details their own way.
I tackle administrative tasks on a regular schedule so they never pile up.
I handle administrative tasks in bursts when they become unavoidable.
I plan social events well ahead of time with confirmed dates and details.
I organize social events spontaneously, often on the same day they happen.
I check my work against established standards or rubrics before considering it done.
I know my work is done when it feels right, not when it passes a formal checklist.
I keep a record of decisions and the reasoning behind them for future reference.
I make decisions and move forward without documenting the rationale behind each one.
I set clear start and end times for work sessions to maintain discipline.
I work until I'm done or until my energy shifts, without fixed start and end times.
I regularly audit my habits to see which ones are still serving me.
I let habits form and dissolve naturally without deliberate auditing.
I test new approaches on a small scale before rolling them out widely.
I implement new approaches fully and adjust based on live results.
I keep a curated collection of reference materials organized by topic.
I find reference materials when I need them rather than curating a collection.
I break vague goals into concrete next actions so I always know what to do next.
I sit with vague goals and let clarity emerge before forcing them into action steps.
I account for travel time, transitions, and setup when planning my schedule.
I tend to underestimate how long transitions take and adjust as I go.
I use consistent formats and templates for recurring communications.
I craft each communication fresh because templates feel impersonal to me.
I track the time I spend on different activities to understand where my hours go.
I spend my time without tracking it, trusting my sense of how the day went.
I establish personal policies for recurring decisions so I don't have to deliberate each time.
I make each recurring decision fresh because circumstances always differ.
I leave detailed notes for my future self when pausing a project mid-stream.
I pick up paused projects and re-orient myself through exploration rather than notes.
I regularly clean out expired items from my refrigerator and pantry.
I notice expired items when I happen to come across them, not on a set schedule.
I prepare agendas before any meeting I'm responsible for.
I let meetings flow conversationally rather than following a prepared agenda.
I choose reliability over novelty when both are available.
I choose novelty over reliability because trying new things is how I learn.
I keep careful records of warranties, receipts, and important transactions.
I deal with warranty and receipt issues when they arise rather than maintaining records.
I build in checkpoints during complex work to verify I'm still on the right track.
I trust my sense of direction during complex work without stopping to verify at fixed points.
I compare my current progress to my original timeline to gauge whether I'm ahead or behind.
I gauge progress by feel rather than comparing actual results to a predetermined timeline.
I plan my learning around specific skill gaps I've identified.
I learn whatever draws my curiosity without mapping it to identified skill gaps.
I treat my future obligations as commitments that deserve present-day preparation.
I give my full attention to what's happening now and prepare for future obligations closer to the time.
I set up automated reminders for bills, renewals, and recurring obligations.
I pay bills and handle obligations when I notice them rather than setting up automation.
I organize information I consume into notes, highlights, or summaries for later use.
I consume information and trust that what matters will stick without explicit note-taking.
I plan my errands in an efficient route rather than running them randomly.
I run errands as they occur to me, sometimes doubling back, and that's fine by me.
I define done criteria for my projects before I start working on them.
I know a project is done when it feels complete, not when it meets predefined criteria.
I maintain a structured approach to professional development with clear learning objectives.
I develop professionally by following whatever interests me at any given time.
I finalize plans early to give myself and others time to prepare.
I finalize plans late because earlier decisions might need changing anyway.
I keep track of favors I owe and make a point of reciprocating promptly.
I reciprocate favors when the opportunity naturally arises rather than tracking them.
I document processes I've figured out so I or others can repeat them.
I figure things out each time rather than writing down how I did it before.
I consolidate similar errands and tasks to minimize wasted time.
I handle errands and tasks as they come up, even if that means extra trips.
I set firm boundaries around my work time to maintain a consistent rhythm.
I let my work time expand and contract naturally with my creative energy.
I think about what skills I'll need in the future and start developing them now.
I develop skills when I actually need them rather than anticipating future requirements.
I review my commitments at the start of each week to ensure nothing is forgotten.
I handle commitments as they surface throughout the week without a formal review.
I stick to proven methods when the stakes are high.
I see high-stakes situations as opportunities to try bold new approaches.
I plan backward from deadlines, identifying each milestone that must be hit along the way.
I work forward from where I am, letting deadlines exert their pull as they approach.
I maintain a clear separation between planning time and execution time.
Planning and execution blend together for me; I think and do simultaneously.
I invest time creating systems that make future work easier even if setup takes a while.
I'd rather start doing the work immediately than spend time building systems first.
I treat my calendar as a binding contract with myself.
I treat my calendar as a rough suggestion that I adjust throughout the day.
I finish unpleasant but necessary tasks promptly so they don't hang over me.
I wait for the right mood to tackle unpleasant tasks, knowing the timing matters.
I create redundancy in important systems so a single failure doesn't cause disaster.
I keep things simple and deal with failures as they happen rather than building in redundancy.
I maintain a consistent work environment because familiarity supports focus.
I change my work environment often because new surroundings stimulate fresh thinking.
I balance short-term demands with long-term priorities using explicit trade-off criteria.
I trust my intuition to balance short-term and long-term needs without formal criteria.
I label storage containers, drawers, and shelves so everything has a designated place.
I put things wherever there's space and rely on my memory to find them.
I complete the hardest tasks first when my energy is highest.
I work on whatever pulls me in, whether it's hard or easy, based on the moment.
I read contracts and agreements thoroughly before signing them.
I skim agreements and sign quickly, trusting I'll address issues if they arise.
I treat small daily choices as building blocks for long-term outcomes.
I treat each day as a standalone experience rather than a chapter in a larger arc.
I confirm appointments the day before to avoid miscommunications.
I assume appointments will happen as planned without confirming them again.
I schedule specific times to check and respond to messages rather than staying reactive.
I check and respond to messages whenever they arrive, staying in the flow of communication.
I weigh the trade-offs of a decision on paper before committing.
I weigh trade-offs in my head quickly and go with what feels best.
I maintain an inventory of supplies so I can reorder before running out.
I notice I'm out of something and get more when I need to.
I build transition rituals between activities to stay grounded and focused.
I move between activities fluidly without needing rituals or transitions.
I take detailed meeting notes and circulate them afterward.
I focus on the conversation during meetings and trust the key points will stick.
I create standard operating procedures for tasks I'll need to repeat.
I approach repeated tasks with fresh eyes each time rather than standardizing them.
I allocate my energy deliberately, doing demanding work when I'm sharpest.
I follow my curiosity regardless of my energy level, trusting engagement will sustain me.
I precommit to decisions to protect my future self from short-sighted impulses.
I keep my options open because my future self may see things differently.
I clean as I go when cooking or working on a project to avoid a big mess at the end.
I focus entirely on the task at hand and clean up everything at the end in one pass.
I evaluate new opportunities against my existing priorities before saying yes.
I say yes to compelling opportunities even if they don't fit my current priorities.
I keep a maintenance schedule for equipment, subscriptions, and recurring needs.
I deal with maintenance needs when they become apparent rather than on a schedule.
I time-box my research phase so I don't lose myself in gathering information.
I research until I feel I have enough rather than imposing time limits on exploration.
I use project management frameworks to coordinate complex work.
I coordinate complex work through conversations and shared intuition rather than frameworks.
I rehearse new procedures mentally before executing them for the first time.
I execute new procedures and learn from the actual experience of doing them.
I set recurring calendar events for tasks that need to happen regularly.
I do regular tasks when I remember them rather than relying on calendar prompts.
I compare estimated time versus actual time on tasks to improve my planning accuracy.
I don't track how long things take because every situation is different anyway.
I make sure my workspace is set up before I begin each work session.
I start working wherever I happen to be without preparing my environment.
I believe that mastery comes from repeating the fundamentals until they are effortless.
I believe that mastery comes from continually pushing into unfamiliar territory.
I write down open questions and revisit them systematically until they're resolved.
I let open questions simmer in the background and trust answers will surface when ready.
I keep a structured record of contacts and when I last communicated with them.
I reconnect with people when the impulse strikes rather than tracking our last contact.
I build buffers into estimates because I know unexpected complications are inevitable.
I give optimistic estimates and adjust timelines if complications actually arise.
I feel that a well-maintained system eventually runs itself, freeing my attention for new challenges.
I feel that maintaining systems takes energy away from the creative work I'd rather do.
I verify that backups of important data are running correctly on a regular basis.
I think about backups only when something goes wrong and I wish I had one.
I set expectations with others about response times so they know when to hear from me.
I respond when I can and trust that people will follow up if something is truly urgent.
I practice new skills in a structured, progressive way, building from simple to complex.
I jump into the deep end with new skills and piece together understanding through experience.
I evaluate whether a task is the best use of my time before doing it.
I do whatever task is in front of me without analyzing whether it's the optimal use of my time.
I write end-of-day summaries to capture what was accomplished and what's pending.
I end my day when I'm done and pick up the thread fresh tomorrow without summarizing.
I use a decision journal to record significant choices and revisit them later.
I make decisions, move on, and rarely look back at how I arrived at them.
I place items back in their designated spots immediately after using them.
I set items down wherever is convenient and tidy up when I feel like it.
I plan my week on Sunday evening so Monday morning starts with clarity.
I let Monday morning unfold and see what the week brings.
I align daily activities with my long-term vision so every day moves me forward.
I let daily activities be guided by what excites me rather than by a long-term vision.
I think of structure as scaffolding that can be removed once the work is solid.
I think of structure as something that should only be added if the work clearly demands it.
I develop contingency plans for critical tasks even when things are going well.
I focus all my energy on the primary plan rather than diverting effort to contingencies.
I verify my understanding by restating instructions before carrying them out.
I act on my initial understanding and ask for clarification only if I get stuck.
I organize my thoughts in writing before tackling a complex problem.
I think best while doing, not while planning on paper.
I maintain an orderly queue of tasks rather than juggling them all at once.
I keep all my tasks in motion simultaneously, moving between them as energy allows.
I resist starting new projects until current ones are at a natural stopping point.
I start new projects whenever inspiration strikes, even if others are unfinished.
I keep communication threads organized so I can retrieve past discussions easily.
I rarely go back to past conversations, preferring to keep things current.
I feel that predictable routines are the foundation on which creative breakthroughs are built.
I feel that breaking routines is what opens the door to creative breakthroughs.
I calibrate my effort to the importance of each task, giving more attention to high-impact work.
I give my full attention to whatever I'm working on regardless of its strategic importance.
I prefer having a detailed recipe or formula before attempting something new.
I prefer to experiment freely, using recipes and formulas only as loose guidelines.
I line up resources and approvals before launching into action.
I start moving and gather resources and approvals along the way.
I design my environment to support my goals, removing temptations and distractions.
I trust myself to stay focused regardless of what's around me.
I consider the downstream effects of my choices on future commitments before deciding.
I make choices based on present circumstances and adapt to downstream effects as they appear.
I rotate seasonal items in and out of storage on a predictable schedule.
I swap seasonal items when the weather actually changes rather than on a set date.
I proofread every piece of writing, even casual messages, before sending.
I send messages quickly and correct mistakes afterward if they matter.
I use deliberate practice techniques when building a new skill.
I build new skills through immersion and play rather than deliberate drill.
I see my daily routine as a series of intentional choices, not defaults.
I see my daily routine as something that emerges from my mood and circumstances.
